title: Handoff ritual — two ownership modes and a public checklist
problem: The portal promises that at the end of any engagement the client owns everything — the code repository, the accounts, the domain. That promise inverts the usual agency lock-in model and self-selects clients who value autonomy, so it is load-bearing positioning. But a promise is only as good as its operational backing. A skeptical buyer — a notary, an accountant, a tradesperson — cannot verify a one-line promise before signing, and the promise said nothing about a buyer who would rather not hold the keys at all, or about what happens if the consultant becomes unreachable. This feature turns the promise into something inspectable: two named ownership modes, a public page that explains them, a public checklist with the exact transfer procedure, and a documented dormancy path the client can execute alone.
analysis: The practice offers two ownership modes rather than one. The default, 'Tout à toi', means the client holds every key from day one. The optional 'Je m'en occupe' means the consultant stays custodian — handling the domain, certificates, and small upkeep — for an annual fee. The managed mode is only honest if it cannot become a new kind of lock-in, so two safeguards are built in: non-payment quietly reverts the client to 'Tout à toi' rather than cutting off service, and a documented dormancy procedure lets the client take ownership unilaterally if the consultant goes silent. The trust move is publishing the operational checklist — every transferable asset, with the exact steps — so a buyer can read the whole transfer procedure before signing rather than taking it on faith.
implementation: Two public pages: src/pages/Handoff.tsx explains the two modes, lists the transferable assets, walks the handoff steps, and covers the switching and dormancy policies; src/pages/HandoffChecklist.tsx is the operational checklist — a plain-language step summary plus a collapsible technical section with the exact commands per asset, including the dormancy procedure. Both are bilingual with local copy and are routed at /handoff and /handoff/checklist (and their /en mirrors). The pricing table surfaces a one-line pointer to /handoff under the managed-mode note, the footer carries a 'how it ends' link site-wide, and the About section adds a short ownership paragraph. The intake form carries an optional, non-blocking ownership-preference radio so a buyer with a clear preference can record it early; the choice is confirmed for real at handoff, not at intake. An offline mirror of the checklist is kept in the repository for clients who want a printable copy.

decisions.decision: Offer two modes instead of a single unconditional 'you own it'
decisions.rationale: Some non-technical buyers explicitly do not want to manage domain renewals, certificates, and security patches — forcing them to own everything just creates a different failure, where the deliverable rots from neglect. A managed mode lets the consultant honestly charge for that custodial labour, and because non-payment reverts to full ownership it does not break the no-lock-in promise.
alternativesConsidered: Single unconditional 'Tout à toi' mode — ignores buyers who want a managed offering
A multi-plan subscription — contradicts the boring-tech and single-operator constraints
Free managed mode — creates a silent backlog of dormant clients still owed renewals

decisions.decision: Publish the operational checklist publicly, not as an internal document
decisions.rationale: The target buyer is skeptical and wants to verify before signing. Publishing the exact transfer procedure makes the no-lock-in promise inspectable; hiding it behind 'ask for details' would make the promise look like marketing theatre.
alternativesConsidered: Internal-only document — the buyer cannot verify without asking
A public page behind a request-access gate — adds friction for no security gain

decisions.decision: Document a dormancy procedure the client can execute alone
decisions.rationale: Managed mode is only honest if the client has a written path to ownership when the consultant is unreachable. Without it, 'Je m'en occupe' is structurally a lock-in regardless of intent. The checklist names the dormancy threshold, the support tickets to open, and the order of operations, and the client holds a printed copy at delivery.
alternativesConsidered: Trust-based, no documented procedure — the client cannot recover ownership
A credential-escrow service — introduces a third party and contradicts the simplicity mandate

decisions.decision: Mode preference is a soft choice at intake, confirmed for real at handoff
decisions.rationale: Forcing the choice at intake friction-loads the form for a visitor who does not yet know what managed mode means. Confirming at handoff means the choice is made after the buyer has seen the deliverable and understood the trade-offs. The intake radio is optional so it never blocks submission.
alternativesConsidered: A required choice at intake — friction without information
No intake field at all — misses buyers who already have a firm preference

successCriteria:
- /handoff explains both ownership modes and reaches the switching and dormancy sections
- /handoff/checklist renders the full transfer procedure, including the dormancy path
- The footer carries a site-wide 'how it ends' link, the pricing table points to /handoff, and the About section mentions ownership
- The intake form shows the optional ownership-preference radio without blocking submission
- A skeptical buyer can verify the no-lock-in promise end to end before signing
userGuide: For a buyer evaluating the practice: read /handoff first to understand the two modes — 'Tout à toi' (you hold every key, free) and 'Je m'en occupe' (the consultant stays custodian for an annual fee). Read /handoff/checklist to verify the exact transfer procedure before signing; the dormancy section documents how to take ownership alone if the consultant becomes unreachable. The ownership-preference radio on the intake form is optional and can be decided later, at delivery.
knownLimitations: The public checklist is hand-mirrored to an offline copy in the repository — the two can drift until a build step generates one from the other
The managed-mode annual price is set without a signed managed client to calibrate against and may need adjustment after the first real engagement
The intake ownership-preference value shows as a friendly label on visitor-facing surfaces but as a raw code in the admin session view
No automated test exercises the dormancy recovery end to end — it depends on third-party support workflows that cannot be safely run in CI
The pricing pointer to /handoff renders only on the managed-eligible tier card; widening managed mode to other tiers would need a rendering change

statusHistory.reason: Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Handoff.feature.json. Re-checked both pages, the routes, the pricing pointer, and the intake radio against the shipped code.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial fill — the two-mode ownership decision, the public-checklist trust strategy, the dormancy procedure, and the soft intake preference.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Simplified the checklist for its real audience — a plain-language step summary plus a single collapsible technical section — and made the procedure mechanics less legalistic.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Code-first overhaul: co-located next to Handoff.tsx, added liveUrl, and tightened the prose so it describes the shipped pages without brittle file-and-line references.
